narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 41% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students with a higher likelihood of passing.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance in Year 1 and Year 3, represented by your decile rankings, were the most significant factors. These rankings were lower than the average, which contributed to the model's prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam. Additionally, your LSAT score, while close to the average, also played a role in the prediction, slightly pushing it towards a potential failure.

Your family income bracket and racial background were also considered, with your middle-income status and being White aligning closely with the majority of applicants. These factors had a moderate influence on the prediction. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was above average, which worked in your favor, suggesting that you have strong academic potential.

Gender and whether you plan to study full-time or part-time had the least influence on the prediction. Being female and studying full-time are common among applicants and did not significantly sway the model's decision.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam in the future, you might focus on enhancing your academic performance in any remaining coursework or consider retaking the LSAT to achieve a higher score. These are areas where you have the potential to make changes that could positively impact your future applications. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive measure of your abilities, and with dedication and effort, you can certainly improve your prospects.
